Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Requiring Plaintiff To File An Undertaking For Security Of Cost And Attorneys Fees Pursuant To Ccp 1030
Off Calendar Jun-04-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Plaintiff ANDREA RICHARDSON (Tel: 510-512-2187) appearing in pro per via CourtCall;
Atty. GREGORY LEWIS, Esq. of Hassard Bonnington LLP (Tel: 415-494-4907) appearing via CourtCall for defendant.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion For An Order Requiring Plaintiff To File An Undertaking For Security Of Cost And Attorneys Fees Pursuant To CCP 1030.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with modifications as follows:

Defendant the Regents of the University of California's motion to require undertaking pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure 1030 is granted. Plaintiff shall post $10,000 as a reasonable bond by July 5, 2020. This is without prejudice to the defendant bringing a further motion under Code of Civil Procedure 996.010 at a later time to show that the set amount of the bond has become insufficient.

Plaintiff resides out of state (Alabama) and defendant demonstrates that "there is a reasonable possibility that the moving defendant will obtain judgment in the action." (Yao v. Superior Court (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 327, 331 (internal quotations omitted).) Defendant shows that it met the standard of care and it did not cause plaintiff's injuries. (Dr. Sundberg Decl.)

In plaintiff's "objection," she does not contest defendant's showing. Rather, she seeks to be excused from the bond requirement because she is destitute. (See Conover v. Hall (1974) 11 Cal.3d 842, 850-851.) Plaintiff's objection, which is devoid of any evidence, does not establish a basis for a waiver. (See Burkes v. Robertson (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 334, 346 ["A party seeking relief under this statute must make a motion to the trial court and has the burden of proving [s]he was in fact indigent."].) Even if the court (which previously granted plaintiff's request for an initial waiver of court fees and costs) assumes that plaintiff is indigent, other factors militate against a waiver, which is discretionary. (See id. ["However, a motion and a declaration of indigency is not sufficient alone to grant a waiver request."]; Code Civ. Proc. 995.240 ["In exercising its discretion the court shall take into consideration all factors it deems relevant, including but not limited to the character of the action or proceeding, the nature of the beneficiary, whether public or private, and the potential harm to the beneficiary if the provision for the bond is waived."].)

This medical malpractice action requires expert testimony. The only testimony before the court shows that defendant did not cause plaintiff's injuries, which arose approximately two years after her liver surgery. (Dr. Sundberg Decl., par. 6(c).)

The Court modifies and signs defendant's proposed order.

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-30-2019 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Andrea Richardson, 510-512-2187 Plaintiff in Pro Per through Court Call.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Discussed. Plaintiff seeks clarification re: next step and accepts the Court's tentative ruling. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendant The Regents of the University of California's demurrer to the first and second causes of action of Plaintiff Andrea Richardson's second amended complaint is sustained. (See Code Civ. Proc., 430.10(e).) Although Plaintiff did not file and serve her opposition at least 9 court days prior to the hearing, as required, and the Court previously warned Plaintiff that failure to comply with the rules could be grounds for sustaining a demurrer without leave to amend and dismissing the action, the Court decides the demurrer on its merits.

The demurrer as to the first cause of action for breach of contract is sustained without leave to amend because the complaint fails to establish the existence of a written contract between the parties. Plaintiff alleges the parties entered into a written contract, but in her opposition she argues instead that "the contract between Defendant was specifically through an implied patient-doctor relationship which would not be required to be in writing." She also refers to medical consent forms, but has not attached them to the second amended complaint or alleged their contents. A doctor's liability under a breach of contract theory is limited. "[A] doctor may be held liable on a theory of breach of contract where he has clearly and unequivocally warranted that a course of treatment recommended by him will, inevitably, produce a certain result." (Pulvers v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (1979) 99 Cal.App.3d 560, 564-565; accord, McKinney v. Nash (1981) 120 Cal.App.3d 428, 442 ["To recover for breach of warranty or contract in a medical malpractice case, there must be proof of an express contract by which the physician clearly promises a particular result and the patient consents to treatment in reliance on that promise. [Citation.] The promise of a particular result is to be distinguished from the mere generalized statement that the result of treatment will be good. [Citation.]]) No such express contract or promise is alleged in the second amended complaint, and plaintiff's allegations regarding what, "as implied," she "understood and expected" defendant to do make it clear that she does not claim any such express contract was entered into.

The demurrer as to the second cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is sustained without leave to amend. The complaint does not allege that Defendant made a false representation as to a past or existing material fact. (See Majd v. Bank of America, N.A. (2015) 243 Cal.App.4th 1293, 1307.) Implied assertions or representations are not enough. (Wilson v. Century 21 Great Western Realty (1993) 15 Cal.App.4th 298, 306 ["Negligent misrepresentation is a species of fraud or deceit specifically requiring a "positive assertion" or "assertion" of fact."]) In her opposition, Plaintiff argues she asked a transplant clinical coordinator before her surgery what would happen if there were complications, and that the coordinator responded, "we'll take care of it." But, to be actionable, a negligent misrepresentation ordinarily must be as to past or existing material facts. (Tarmann v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. (1991) 2 Cal.App.4th 153, 158 ["[P]redictions as to future events, or statements as to future action by some third party, are deemed opinions, and not actionable fraud."].)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
